message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html
**Title:** The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (full article, Samuel Proulx)
**Found via:** formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract claims relevant to: why blind/screen-reader power users prefer formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural TTS at high speed (18-22 syllables/sec); intelligibility vs naturalness trade-off; latency/responsiveness of neural vs formant TTS; consistency/predictability of synthesizers at speed; speaking rate; any AI/neural TTS limitations for fast listening. Quote exact sentences. Also note the publish date and author.

content.content:
# Claims Extract: Screen Reader User TTS Preferences

**Author:** Samuel Proulx  
**Published:** January 5, 2026

## Preference for Formant Synthesizers Over Neural TTS

**Intelligibility vs. Naturalness Trade-off:**
- "blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic"
- "While sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible" the needs differ fundamentally

**Speaking Rate Capability:**
- "blind users...can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute"
- "The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison"

## Neural TTS Limitations

**Latency/Responsiveness:**
- "Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text"
- "a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible...Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt"

**Consistency/Predictability:**
- "AI text to speech models...cannot offer this customization...they inherit the speaking speed, pitch, volume...from training data"
- "Kitten TTS and Supertonic both offer basic speed control, however it is highly variable from voice to voice"

**Accuracy Issues:**
- Models "had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances" — "unacceptable" for screen reader use

content.text: Let me get more detail on specific quantified claims and exact wording.

input.prompt: Quote the EXACT sentences (verbatim, word-for-word) from the article about: (1) the words-per-minute speeds blind users listen at and average speech rate; (2) why low latency / immediate start of speech generation matters for screen readers and how neural models fail this; (3) any statement about responsiveness, interrupting, or jumping through text; (4) accuracy problems like skipping words or misreading numbers; (5) speed/pitch/volume customization that traditional synthesizers offer but neural ones do not. Give me the precise verbatim sentences with surrounding context.

content.content:
# Key Quotes from the Article

**(1) Words-per-minute speeds:**
"blind folks have remained mostly static... blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient... often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison."

**(2) Low latency importance and neural model failure:**
"Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text... for use in a screen reader, a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible, rather than waiting for an entire phrase or sentence."

**(3) Interrupting and jumping through text:**
"Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech."

**(4) Accuracy problems:**
"both models had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints from text punctuation."

**(5) Customization capabilities:**
"Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy... AI text to speech models... cannot offer this customization."

content.text: I have enough verbatim quotes to extract falsifiable claims. The source is a detailed blog/opinion piece by Samuel Proulx (an accessibility expert), reflecting authoritative practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader community.

claims.claim: Blind/screen-reader power users prefer voices that are robotic-sounding because they prioritize speed, clarity, predictability, and efficiency over naturalness, the opposite of sighted users' preferences.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic

claims.claim: Experienced blind listeners comprehend speech at roughly 800-900 words per minute, about 4x the average human speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, supporting the need for synthesizers optimized for extreme speed.
claims.quote: often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison.

claims.claim: Neural TTS models (Supertonic, Kitten TTS) cannot stream incrementally because they require an entire text chunk before generating, whereas screen readers need speech to begin immediately, a latency/responsiveness disadvantage versus formant synthesizers.
claims.quote: Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text... for use in a screen reader, a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible, rather than waiting for an entire phrase or sentence.

claims.claim: Screen reader users frequently interrupt and jump through text, requiring the TTS to discard and restart speech instantly, a real-time responsiveness requirement neural models struggle to meet.
claims.quote: Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech.

claims.claim: Older formant synthesizers expose granular voice parameters (pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize) that neural TTS models cannot, because neural models inherit fixed prosody from training data.
claims.quote: Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy... AI text to speech models... cannot offer this customization.

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from Samuel Proulx's article (published 2026-01-05). The source is a blog/opinion piece, but authoritative practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader accessibility space. Key central claims cover the robotic-but-clear preference of blind power users, the 800-900 wpm listening rate (vs 200-250 wpm average), and the streaming/latency disadvantage of neural TTS (Supertonic, Kitten TTS) that require full text chunks before generating. Supporting claims cover interrupt/restart responsiveness needs and the loss of granular voice-parameter control in neural models.
